messages.text: Do we have a signed cooperate agreement with the NYC DOT?

This what I know: (Do not shoot the messenger, I am just stating the facts I know)
• The project materialized in like a month or so, as USB used there leverage to get the project going. 
    ◦ I highly doubt they got a cooperative agreement reviewed and done in a month. 
    ◦ I could be wrong though.
• This was one of the last projects I handed off before I left September 11th, well as much as a hand off as there could be. 
    ◦ The project was a Colorbiotics project as it happened in September (9/15-9/28), and NYC/ Karen wanted a commercial entity that could supply the NYC market. (that is why it wasn't an ISU project)
    ◦ From what the contracts and what Marv said CB was at the front of this, so if there was a Cooperate agreement signed you might have to ask CB.
• Irene pretty much said play ball or the city wont use your product ever. 
    ◦ Rutgers had to be used for this project
    ◦ No NDA was signed 
I wanted an NDA, as I had flash backs about the Heartland Project.

Rutgers is noted on the Sripath website as a research partner

Thus I would not consider Rutgers to be a fully unbiased participant
Did we know that ahead of time?
No, I guess but I guess we did not check for conflict of interest either.
I don't know that they were forthcoming about who we were being compared to
The company’s headquarters is strategically located 40 minutes by car from the Rutger’s campus.
Clearly these guys are an almost exact competitor...we have know our competition inside out...
it looks like they are mainly on the east coast, but clearly they've done something right to get on the in with NYC
Eric that’s a conversation with city asphalt, as they beat out Cargill
Old market, old buildings...lots of flat roofs...highly connected politically (to get a sale)...not really our geographic competitor...if I'm assessng opportunities...NYC is way down on the list

When I get a panicked phone call about our test section having low density below 100lbs/cubic foot from nuclear gauge readings. I hope this information is in their report. A person must be certified to use a nuclear gauge and most often by a State DOT. The typical density coming off the back of the paving machine before a roller hits it is 120 lbs/cubic foot.
Jim, I would agree. I was overridden on the the project, we can’t really service them effectively being in NYC.
Can’t change the past but we should make improvements going forward.
This guy is "liking" Adventus posts on LinkedIn... Be advised
